concepts.title: Claisen Rearrangement of Aryl Allyl Ethers
Reactivity of Ethers
concepts.conceptBody:
Reactivity:

  • The ethereal $O$ atom is a region of high electron density due to the lone pairs.
  • Ether oxygen atoms are Lewis bases.
  • Like an alcohol $-\text{OH}$ group, the $-\text{OR}$ group is a poor leaving group and needs to be converted to a better leaving group before substitution can occur.
  • The most important reaction of ethers is their cleavage by strong acids such as $\text{HI}$ or $\text{HBr}$.

concepts.title: Cleavage of Ether by Acids
concepts.conceptBody:
Reactions Of Ethers

Ethers are relatively inert compounds and do not react with alkalies, reducing agent, oxidizing agents and reactive metals. Because of their inertness ethers are frequently used as solvents. They, however, undergo cleavage under acidic conditions.

Cleavage by acids 

On reaction with conc. $\text{HBr}$ or conc. $\text{HI}$, ethers undergo cleavage to form an alcohol or phenol and an alkyl halide.

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}-\text{O}-{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}+\text{HX}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{X}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}$

  • The order of reactivity of halogen acids is

$\text{HI}>\text{HBr}>\text{HCl}$

  • In case of excess acid, product formed is only alkyl halide

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}-\text{O}-{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}+\underset{\text{ excess }}{\mathop{2\text{HX}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{X}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Mechanism: 

Step I:

Step II: 

So, reaction can go by ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}1$ or ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}2$ depending upon the conditions employed and the structure of ether. When both the alkyl groups are methyl or 1 , it will follow ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}2$ pathway and when atleast one of the alkyl group is 3 , it follows ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}1$ pathway.

For example,

(a)

(b)

Note: When the reaction follows ${{S}_{N}}2$ mechanism $\left( R>{{R}^{\prime }} \right)$ then ${{I}^{-}}$attacks ${{R}^{\prime }}$ and forms ${{R}^{\prime }}I$ and ROH.

But, if ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}1$ mechanism is followed than RI and ${{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\text{OH}$ is formed.

In acidic medium, in case of cleavage of ether, if strong nucleophile is used, then reaction takes place by ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}2$ mechanism.

concepts.title: Reaction of Ether with PCl5/SOCl2
concepts.conceptBody:
Reaction with $\mathbf{PC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{5}}}\mathbf{/SOC}{{\mathbf{l}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

Ethers on heating with $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}$ or $\text{SOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ undergoes cleavage to form alkyl chlorides.

The cleavage by $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}$ or $\text{SOC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is and to distinguish metameric ethers as their cleavage.

concepts.title: Oxidation of Ether by Air
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidation of Ether by Air

When ethers are exposed to oxygen for a long time they are oxidised to ether hydroperoxides (solid) which explodes on slightest heating or on rubbing.

Ethers are purified by extraction with excess of $\text{FeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ solution, which reduces the $-\text{OOH}$ gap to the non-explosive, innocuous - $\text{OH}$ group.

concepts.title: Test of Peroxide Ion
concepts.conceptBody:
Test of Peroxide ion

When ether hydroperoxide is reduced with $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+2}}$, it oxidises $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+2}}$ to $\text{F}{{\text{e}}^{+3}}$ which can easily be detected by thiocyanide ion producing green colour.
